message.content:
In the Rust repo at /home/wink/data/prgs/rust/vc-x1, catalog information for a planned refactor. Two tasks, report raw findings:

1. Single-field options_flags leaves. The convention exemplar is src/options_flags/squash.rs, where the struct's single field is named `value` (flag name via `#[arg(long = "...")]`). The following leaves still name the field after the flag: repo (src/options_flags/repo.rs), dry_run, private, account, config (src/options_flags/config.rs), use_template. For EACH of these six leaves report:
   - the struct name and its current field name + type (read the leaf file)
   - every consumer site outside the leaf file: grep for field accesses like `.dry_run`, `.private`, `.account`, `.use_template`, and for repo.rs and config.rs read the file first to learn the struct/field names then grep their accesses. Report file:line and the expression (e.g. `args.dry_run.dry_run`). Be careful to distinguish accesses of the leaf struct field from unrelated identically-named locals/fields — note ambiguous ones.
   - whether the leaf has `#[arg(long)]` inferring the flag name from the field (renaming the field to `value` then requires adding explicit `long = "..."`).

2. Also read src/options_flags/README.md and src/options_flags/mod.rs and report what they say about the single-field `value` convention (quote the relevant passages).

Return a structured plain-text report. Do not modify any files.

attachment.content:
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact page can be granted — behavior static HTML cannot provide on its own, such as the page reading live or connected data, keeping state shared across viewers, or updating and republishing itself. Serves this user's live capability roster and the typed call definitions. Load it whenever the user asks for an artifact needing any such runtime behavior.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- claude-in-chrome: Automates your Chrome browser to interact with web pages - clicking elements, filling forms, capturing screenshots, reading console logs, and navigating sites. Opens pages in new tabs within your existing Chrome session. Requires site-level permissions before executing (configured in the extension). - When the user wants to interact with web pages, automate browser tasks, capture screenshots, read console logs, or perform any browser-based actions. Always invoke BEFORE attempting to use any mcp__claude-in-chrome__* tools.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll help you catalog information for the refactor. Let me systematically explore the options_flags directory and examine each file.

input.description: List Rust files in options_flags directory

content.content:
1	//! `--dry-run` — show what would be done without executing.
2	//! See [options_flags](README.md) for shared architecture.
3	
4	use clap::Args;
5	
6	/// `--dry-run` leaf (Flag — boolean domain) — see
7	/// [Consuming an OF](README.md#consuming-an-of).
8	#[derive(Args, Debug, Clone, Default)]
9	pub struct DryRunFlag {
10	    /// Dry run — show what would be done without executing.
11	    #[arg(long)]
12	    pub dry_run: bool,
13	}
14

1	//! `--private` — create private repos on the remote provisioner.
2	//! See [options_flags](README.md) for shared architecture.
3	
4	use clap::Args;
5	
6	/// `--private` leaf (Flag — boolean domain) — see
7	/// [Consuming an OF](README.md#consuming-an-of).
8	#[derive(Args, Debug, Clone, Default)]
9	pub struct PrivateFlag {
10	    /// Create private GitHub repos (default: public).
11	    ///
12	    /// - Only meaningful when the resolved provisioner is
13	    ///   `gh repo create` (GitHub URL or `--repo remote` whose
14	    ///   value points at GitHub).
15	    #[arg(long, verbatim_doc_comment)]
16	    pub private: bool,
17	}
18

1	//! `--account` — selects an account section in the user config.
2	//! See [options_flags](README.md) for shared architecture.
3	
4	use clap::Args;
5	
6	/// `--account` leaf (Option — non-boolean domain) — see
7	/// [Consuming an OF](README.md#consuming-an-of).
8	#[derive(Args, Debug, Clone, Default)]
9	pub struct AccountOption {
10	    /// Account name — picks `[account.<a>]` from user config.
11	    ///
12	    /// - Without this flag, `[default].account` (or top-level
13	    ///   `[repo]` shorthand) is used.
14	    /// - Meaningful only with Path or bare-NAME targets — URL /
15	    ///   owner/name targets supply the remote directly.
16	    #[arg(long, value_name = "NAME", verbatim_doc_comment)]
17	    pub account: Option<String>,
18	}
19

1	//! `--use-template` — seed repos from template directories.
2	//! See [options_flags](README.md) for shared architecture.
3	
4	use clap::Args;
5	
6	/// `--use-template` leaf (Option — non-boolean domain) — see
7	/// [Consuming an OF](README.md#consuming-an-of).
8	#[derive(Args, Debug, Clone, Default)]
9	pub struct UseTemplateOption {
10	    /// Seed repos from template directories.
11	    ///
12	    /// Value is `CODE[,BOT]`. Default bot path is `<CODE>.claude`
13	    /// (file-name concat, not path join — templates are siblings).
14	    ///
15	    /// - With `--scope=por`: only `CODE` is used; passing `,BOT`
16	    ///   is fatal (no session side to seed).
17	    /// - Non-hidden contents copied recursively; hidden entries
18	    ///   (names starting with `.`) are skipped — init writes its
19	    ///   own hidden files.
20	    /// - If a copied tree has a `README.md`, its first line is
21	    ///   rewritten to `# <repo-name>`.
22	    #[arg(long, value_name = "CODE[,BOT]", verbatim_doc_comment)]
23	    pub use_template: Option<String>,
24	}
25

1	# options_flags — Reusable CLI Options and Flags
2	
3	Per-flag CLI surfaces shared across multiple subcommands. Each
4	shared option or flag (OF) lives in its own submodule so its
5	type, parser, and tests stay together.
6	
7	This module is the CLI-layer leaf store for the options_flags
8	extraction in [`../../ARCHITECTURE.md`](../../ARCHITECTURE.md);
9	see there for how the leaves fit the wider clap-args /
10	`Context`+`Params` split.
11	
12	## Architecture
13	
14	Three composition patterns:
15	
16	- **Leaf** — a `#[derive(Args)]` struct holding the flag(s),
17	  `value_parser`, default, and help text (via the field
18	  doc-comment). One `#[derive(Args)]` per logical OF — single
19	  flag or a small fixed pair (e.g. `push_retry`'s two fields).
20	  Help text aims to be generic enough for any reasonable
21	  consumer.
22	- **Bundle** — a `#[derive(Args)]` shared by N subcommands,
23	  picked up with one `#[command(flatten)]` line. Two forms:
24	  - *flatten-of-leaves* — composes existing leaves into a named
25	    role (e.g. `ProvisionOptionFlagBundle` = `dry_run` +
26	    `private` + `push_retry`). Use this when the constituent
27	    flags are themselves reused elsewhere.
28	  - *inline-fields* — holds the `#[arg]` fields directly (e.g.
29	    `common_args::CommonArgs` — the read-only commit-query arg
30	    set). Use this when a whole *set* of args is shared but the
31	    individual flags aren't reused outside the bundle —
32	    extracting per-flag leaves would buy no reuse. If one of the
33	    fields ever *is* reused (a future command wants just that
34	    flag), extract it into a leaf then and
35	    `#[command(flatten)]` it into the bundle.
36	- **Pattern A escape hatch** — when a consumer needs unique help
37	  text (or different defaults), it skips the leaf's flatten and
38	  inlines its own `#[arg(value_parser = …)]` field, reusing the
39	  leaf's typed value and parser.
40	
41	## Flag vs Option — classify by domain, not wire syntax
42	
43	Every leaf falls into one of two categories, picked by the value
44	domain it carries:
45	
46	- **Flag** — boolean domain. Wire form may be presence/absence
47	  (`--dry-run`), explicit negation (`--no-dry-run`), or value
48	  (`--dry-run=true|false`). All three express a `bool`. Type
49	  name suffix: `*Flag`.
50	- **Option** — non-boolean domain (string, integer, enum, path,
51	  …). Wire form is always `--name=<value>` (or `--name <value>`).
52	  Type name suffix: `*Option` (or `*Options` for a multi-field
53	  leaf like `PushRetryOptions`).
54	
55	Wire form is presentation; the category is the underlying domain.
56	
57	## Adding a new leaf
58	
59	1. Create `options_flags/<name>.rs` with a
60	   `#[derive(Args, Debug, Clone, Default)]` struct.
61	2. Pick the suffix per domain: `*Flag` (boolean) or `*Option(s)`
62	   (non-boolean).
63	3. Name the field(s):
64	   - **Single-field leaf** — call the field `value` (it holds the
65	     parsed value-side of the option) and declare the flag
66	     explicitly with `#[arg(long = "<flag>", …)]`. The consumer
67	     then reads `args.<leaf>.value` rather than doubling the flag
68	     name (`args.squash.value`, not `args.squash.squash`).
69	     Caveat: clap derives an arg's *id* from the field name, so
70	     if two `value`-field leaves can ever be flattened into the
71	     same struct, give each an explicit `#[arg(id = "<unique>")]`
72	     (else `clap_builder` panics — duplicate arg id `value`).
73	   - **Multi-field leaf** — descriptive per-field names
74	     (`push_retries`, `push_retry_delay`); clap derives each
75	     flag from its field name as usual.
76	   - The pre-existing single-field leaves (`scope`, `repo`,
77	     `dry_run`, `private`, `account`, `config`, `use_template`)
78	     predate this and still derive the flag from the field name;
79	     migrating them to `value` is tracked in
80	     [`../../TODO.md`](../../TODO.md).
81	4. Add `pub mod <name>;` to `mod.rs`.
82	5. If the leaf has explicit parsing logic, declare a unit-struct
83	   implementor of `FlagParser` (boolean domain) or `OptionParser`
84	   (non-boolean domain). Bare `Option<String>` leaves and
85	   presence/absence boolean leaves need no parser impl — clap
86	   handles them.
87	6. Add tests for any non-trivial parser/resolver logic.
88	7. Leaves do **not** implement a Bundle marker — bundles do.
89	
90	## Consuming an OF
91	
92	Default — flatten the leaf into your subcommand's `Args`:
93	
94	```rust
95	#[derive(Args)]
96	pub struct MyArgs {
97	    #[command(flatten)]
98	    pub config: ConfigOption,
99	    // ...
100	}
101	```
102	
103	Pattern A — when generic help doesn't fit:
104	
105	```rust
106	#[derive(Args)]
107	pub struct MyArgs {
108	    /// My subcommand-specific help for --config.
109	    #[arg(long = "config", value_name = "none|PATH",
110	          verbatim_doc_comment)]
111	    pub config: Option<String>,
112	    // ...
113	}
114	```
115	
116	A Pattern A consumer reuses the leaf's types (e.g. `ConfigKind`)
117	and parsers (e.g. `parse_config_kind`) but owns its own clap
118	attributes.
119	
120	## Consumer function shape
121	
122	Helper functions called by a subcommand body should accept the
123	relevant leaf type by reference rather than unpacking individual
124	fields at the call site:
125	
126	```rust
127	// Multi-field leaf — pass the whole leaf
128	fn run_retry(cmd: &str, args: &[&str], cwd: &Path,
129	             retry: &PushRetryOptions) -> Result<…> { … }
130	
131	run_retry("git", &["push", …], cwd, &args.provision.push_retry)?;
132	```
133	
134	This wins on:
135	- Readability — `&args.provision.push_retry` reads as a single
136	  unit; the function body works with `retry.push_retries` (no
137	  leaf-name doubling because the parameter name is the consumer's
138	  choice).
139	- Argument count — every multi-field leaf collapses N args into
140	  one ref, so chained helpers don't accumulate
141	  `clippy::too_many_arguments` warnings.
142	- Future-proofing — extending a leaf with another field is a
143	  zero-touch change at every call site.
144	
145	For **single-field leaves** (e.g. `SquashOption`, `DryRunFlag`,
146	`PrivateFlag`), direct read at the consumer site
147	(`args.squash.value`, or `args.provision.dry_run.dry_run` for the
148	pre-`value` leaves) is fine — wrapping the inner value in a
149	`&LeafType` parameter doesn't earn the indirection.
150	
151	## Marker traits
152	
153	Three Bundle markers — one per content classification. Bundles
154	implement exactly one. Leaves do **not** implement these markers
155	(their category lives in the type-name suffix).
156	
157	- `FlagBundle: clap::Args` — pure-boolean bundle (every
158	  constituent leaf is a Flag). Rare in practice.
159	- `OptionBundle: clap::Args` — pure-non-boolean bundle (every
160	  constituent leaf is an Option).
161	- `OptionFlagBundle: clap::Args` — mixed bundle (constituents
162	  include both Flag and Option leaves). Most common in practice.
163	
164	Two Parser traits — conditional contracts implemented only when
165	a leaf has explicit parsing logic (custom `value_parser`):
166	
167	- `FlagParser` — boolean-domain parser. Implemented only for
168	  boolean leaves that take a value form (`--flag=true|false`).
169	  Presence/absence flags need no impl; clap parses directly.
170	- `OptionParser` — non-boolean-domain parser. Implemented only
171	  for leaves with custom parsing logic (e.g. `RepoParser`);
172	  bare `Option<String>` leaves need no impl.
173	
174	`FlagParser` and `OptionParser` share an identical method shape;
175	the difference is documentation (which value-domain the parser
176	targets).
177	
178	All five traits are documentation-level — clap derive doesn't
179	see them; the discipline is that every leaf and bundle declares
180	the appropriate ones explicitly.
181	
182	## Bundle marker discipline
183	
184	Bundle markers aren't compiler-checked against contents. If a
185	bundle's leaf set shifts category — e.g. a pure-Flag bundle gains
186	an Option leaf — you must update the marker by hand
187	(`FlagBundle` → `OptionFlagBundle`). No lint, no failure; silent
188	drift if forgotten. Worth a glance at the marker line whenever a
189	bundle's `#[command(flatten)]` set changes.
190	
191	## Pattern A worked example
192	
193	`init`'s `--config` field originally carried init-specific help
194	("Only valid with `--scope=por`..."). It currently flattens
195	`ConfigOption` for the generic help; the `--scope=por` constraint
196	surfaces in `init`'s preflight error. If the help-text generality
197	becomes a usability problem, init will switch to Pattern A as the
198	worked example.
199	
200	## Layout note
201	
202	OFs currently sit as flat `<name>.rs` files alongside this
203	README. Bundle modules carry the `_bundle` suffix
204	(e.g. `provision_bundle.rs`) so the leaf/bundle split is visible
205	in the directory listing without opening files. If an individual
206	OF accumulates enough rationale, edge-case detail, or examples
207	to outgrow doc-comments, it will graduate to a `<name>/mod.rs` +
208	`<name>/README.md` subdirectory layout. Mechanical when needed;
209	not done preemptively.
210

file.content:
# options_flags — Reusable CLI Options and Flags

Per-flag CLI surfaces shared across multiple subcommands. Each
shared option or flag (OF) lives in its own submodule so its
type, parser, and tests stay together.

This module is the CLI-layer leaf store for the options_flags
extraction in [`../../ARCHITECTURE.md`](../../ARCHITECTURE.md);
see there for how the leaves fit the wider clap-args /
`Context`+`Params` split.

## Architecture

Three composition patterns:

- **Leaf** — a `#[derive(Args)]` struct holding the flag(s),
  `value_parser`, default, and help text (via the field
  doc-comment). One `#[derive(Args)]` per logical OF — single
  flag or a small fixed pair (e.g. `push_retry`'s two fields).
  Help text aims to be generic enough for any reasonable
  consumer.
- **Bundle** — a `#[derive(Args)]` shared by N subcommands,
  picked up with one `#[command(flatten)]` line. Two forms:
  - *flatten-of-leaves* — composes existing leaves into a named
    role (e.g. `ProvisionOptionFlagBundle` = `dry_run` +
    `private` + `push_retry`). Use this when the constituent
    flags are themselves reused elsewhere.
  - *inline-fields* — holds the `#[arg]` fields directly (e.g.
    `common_args::CommonArgs` — the read-only commit-query arg
    set). Use this when a whole *set* of args is shared but the
    individual flags aren't reused outside the bundle —
    extracting per-flag leaves would buy no reuse. If one of the
    fields ever *is* reused (a future command wants just that
    flag), extract it into a leaf then and
    `#[command(flatten)]` it into the bundle.
- **Pattern A escape hatch** — when a consumer needs unique help
  text (or different defaults), it skips the leaf's flatten and
  inlines its own `#[arg(value_parser = …)]` field, reusing the
  leaf's typed value and parser.

## Flag vs Option — classify by domain, not wire syntax

Every leaf falls into one of two categories, picked by the value
domain it carries:

- **Flag** — boolean domain. Wire form may be presence/absence
  (`--dry-run`), explicit negation (`--no-dry-run`), or value
  (`--dry-run=true|false`). All three express a `bool`. Type
  name suffix: `*Flag`.
- **Option** — non-boolean domain (string, integer, enum, path,
  …). Wire form is always `--name=<value>` (or `--name <value>`).
  Type name suffix: `*Option` (or `*Options` for a multi-field
  leaf like `PushRetryOptions`).

Wire form is presentation; the category is the underlying domain.

## Adding a new leaf

1. Create `options_flags/<name>.rs` with a
   `#[derive(Args, Debug, Clone, Default)]` struct.
2. Pick the suffix per domain: `*Flag` (boolean) or `*Option(s)`
   (non-boolean).
3. Name the field(s):
   - **Single-field leaf** — call the field `value` (it holds the
     parsed value-side of the option) and declare the flag
     explicitly with `#[arg(long = "<flag>", …)]`. The consumer
     then reads `args.<leaf>.value` rather than doubling the flag
     name (`args.squash.value`, not `args.squash.squash`).
     Caveat: clap derives an arg's *id* from the field name, so
     if two `value`-field leaves can ever be flattened into the
     same struct, give each an explicit `#[arg(id = "<unique>")]`
     (else `clap_builder` panics — duplicate arg id `value`).
   - **Multi-field leaf** — descriptive per-field names
     (`push_retries`, `push_retry_delay`); clap derives each
     flag from its field name as usual.
   - The pre-existing single-field leaves (`scope`, `repo`,
     `dry_run`, `private`, `account`, `config`, `use_template`)
     predate this and still derive the flag from the field name;
     migrating them to `value` is tracked in
     [`../../TODO.md`](../../TODO.md).
4. Add `pub mod <name>;` to `mod.rs`.
5. If the leaf has explicit parsing logic, declare a unit-struct
   implementor of `FlagParser` (boolean domain) or `OptionParser`
   (non-boolean domain). Bare `Option<String>` leaves and
   presence/absence boolean leaves need no parser impl — clap
   handles them.
6. Add tests for any non-trivial parser/resolver logic.
7. Leaves do **not** implement a Bundle marker — bundles do.

## Consuming an OF

Default — flatten the leaf into your subcommand's `Args`:

```rust
#[derive(Args)]
pub struct MyArgs {
    #[command(flatten)]
    pub config: ConfigOption,
    // ...
}
```

Pattern A — when generic help doesn't fit:

```rust
#[derive(Args)]
pub struct MyArgs {
    /// My subcommand-specific help for --config.
    #[arg(long = "config", value_name = "none|PATH",
          verbatim_doc_comment)]
    pub config: Option<String>,
    // ...
}
```

A Pattern A consumer reuses the leaf's types (e.g. `ConfigKind`)
and parsers (e.g. `parse_config_kind`) but owns its own clap
attributes.

## Consumer function shape

Helper functions called by a subcommand body should accept the
relevant leaf type by reference rather than unpacking individual
fields at the call site:

```rust
// Multi-field leaf — pass the whole leaf
fn run_retry(cmd: &str, args: &[&str], cwd: &Path,
             retry: &PushRetryOptions) -> Result<…> { … }

run_retry("git", &["push", …], cwd, &args.provision.push_retry)?;
```

This wins on:
- Readability — `&args.provision.push_retry` reads as a single
  unit; the function body works with `retry.push_retries` (no
  leaf-name doubling because the parameter name is the consumer's
  choice).
- Argument count — every multi-field leaf collapses N args into
  one ref, so chained helpers don't accumulate
  `clippy::too_many_arguments` warnings.
- Future-proofing — extending a leaf with another field is a
  zero-touch change at every call site.

For **single-field leaves** (e.g. `SquashOption`, `DryRunFlag`,
`PrivateFlag`), direct read at the consumer site
(`args.squash.value`, or `args.provision.dry_run.dry_run` for the
pre-`value` leaves) is fine — wrapping the inner value in a
`&LeafType` parameter doesn't earn the indirection.

## Marker traits

Three Bundle markers — one per content classification. Bundles
implement exactly one. Leaves do **not** implement these markers
(their category lives in the type-name suffix).

- `FlagBundle: clap::Args` — pure-boolean bundle (every
  constituent leaf is a Flag). Rare in practice.
- `OptionBundle: clap::Args` — pure-non-boolean bundle (every
  constituent leaf is an Option).
- `OptionFlagBundle: clap::Args` — mixed bundle (constituents
  include both Flag and Option leaves). Most common in practice.

Two Parser traits — conditional contracts implemented only when
a leaf has explicit parsing logic (custom `value_parser`):

- `FlagParser` — boolean-domain parser. Implemented only for
  boolean leaves that take a value form (`--flag=true|false`).
  Presence/absence flags need no impl; clap parses directly.
- `OptionParser` — non-boolean-domain parser. Implemented only
  for leaves with custom parsing logic (e.g. `RepoParser`);
  bare `Option<String>` leaves need no impl.

`FlagParser` and `OptionParser` share an identical method shape;
the difference is documentation (which value-domain the parser
targets).

All five traits are documentation-level — clap derive doesn't
see them; the discipline is that every leaf and bundle declares
the appropriate ones explicitly.

## Bundle marker discipline

Bundle markers aren't compiler-checked against contents. If a
bundle's leaf set shifts category — e.g. a pure-Flag bundle gains
an Option leaf — you must update the marker by hand
(`FlagBundle` → `OptionFlagBundle`). No lint, no failure; silent
drift if forgotten. Worth a glance at the marker line whenever a
bundle's `#[command(flatten)]` set changes.

## Pattern A worked example

`init`'s `--config` field originally carried init-specific help
("Only valid with `--scope=por`..."). It currently flattens
`ConfigOption` for the generic help; the `--scope=por` constraint
surfaces in `init`'s preflight error. If the help-text generality
becomes a usability problem, init will switch to Pattern A as the
worked example.

## Layout note

OFs currently sit as flat `<name>.rs` files alongside this
README. Bundle modules carry the `_bundle` suffix
(e.g. `provision_bundle.rs`) so the leaf/bundle split is visible
in the directory listing without opening files. If an individual
OF accumulates enough rationale, edge-case detail, or examples
to outgrow doc-comments, it will graduate to a `<name>/mod.rs` +
`<name>/README.md` subdirectory layout. Mechanical when needed;
not done preemptively.

input.description: Find accesses to .repo field outside repo.rs
Find accesses to .dry_run field outside dry_run.rs
Find accesses to .private field outside private.rs
Find accesses to .account field outside account.rs
Find accesses to .use_template field outside use_template.rs
Find accesses to .raw field outside config.rs (filtering for likely field accesses)

content.content:
1	//! Flat plain-struct input to the init op.
2	//!
3	//! `InitParams` is the clap-free shape that `pub fn init` reads
4	//! from. Built `From<&InitArgs>` at the binary edge (main.rs);
5	//! callers without clap (tests, future TUI / library embedding)
6	//! construct it directly.
7	//!
8	//! Per `notes/chores/chores-09.md > ## InitParams implementation
9	//! (0.44.0)`: domain types pass through (`RepoSelector`,
10	//! `ConfigKind`, `PushRetryOptions`); leaf clap wrappers do not.
11	
12	use crate::config::RepoSelector;
13	use crate::init::InitArgs;
14	use crate::options_flags::config::ConfigKind;
15	use crate::options_flags::push_retry::PushRetryOptions;
16	
17	/// Inputs to the init op, flat, owned, clap-free.
18	///
19	/// - `target`: the TARGET positional argument (URL, owner/name,
20	///   path, or bare NAME).
21	/// - `name`: optional `NAME` positional override.
22	/// - `account`: `--account` value (None ⇒ default account chain).
23	/// - `repo`: `--repo` value parsed into a `RepoSelector`.
24	/// - `por`: `--por` resolved — `true` for plain single repo,
25	///   `false` (default) for dual workspace.
26	/// - `private`: `--private`.
27	/// - `dry_run`: `--dry-run`.
28	/// - `push_retry`: `--push-retries` / `--push-retry-delay`.
29	/// - `use_template`: `--use-template` value.
30	/// - `config`: resolved `--config` value (None ⇒ canned write).
31	/// - `create_symlink`: whether `init` should create the
32	///   `~/.claude/projects/` symlink for dual runs (production
33	///   path = true; test fixtures suppress with false).
34	pub struct InitParams {
35	    pub target: String,
36	    pub name: Option<String>,
37	    pub account: Option<String>,
38	    pub repo: Option<RepoSelector>,
39	    pub por: bool,
40	    pub private: bool,
41	    pub dry_run: bool,
42	    pub push_retry: PushRetryOptions,
43	    pub use_template: Option<String>,
44	    pub config: Option<ConfigKind>,
45	    pub create_symlink: bool,
46	}
47	
48	impl From<&InitArgs> for InitParams {
49	    /// Convert clap-derived `InitArgs` into the flat `InitParams`.
50	    ///
51	    /// Production path: sets `create_symlink: true`. Tests that
52	    /// need `create_symlink: false` construct `InitParams`
53	    /// directly (or post-edit the field).
54	    fn from(a: &InitArgs) -> Self {
55	        Self {
56	            target: a.target.clone(),
57	            name: a.name.clone(),
58	            account: a.account.account.clone(),
59	            repo: a.repo.repo.clone(),
60	            por: a.por.value,
61	            private: a.provision.private.private,
62	            dry_run: a.provision.dry_run.dry_run,
63	            push_retry: a.provision.push_retry.clone(),
64	            use_template: a.use_template.use_template.clone(),
65	            config: a.config.resolve(ConfigKind::None),
66	            create_symlink: true,
67	        }
68	    }
69	}
70

---

## REFACTOR CATALOG REPORT

### TASK 1: SINGLE-FIELD OPTIONS_FLAGS LEAVES — NAMING CONVENTION AUDIT

#### EXEMPLAR: squash.rs (convention reference)
- **Struct**: SquashOption
- **Field**: `value: Option<SquashSpec>` (line 68)
- **Flag declaration**: `#[arg(long = "squash", ...)]` (explicit, line 62)
- **Notes**: Follows the new convention — field named `value`, flag name from explicit `long` attribute. Consumers read `args.squash.value`.

---

#### LEAF 1: repo.rs — DEVIATES FROM CONVENTION

**Struct and field:**
- **Struct**: RepoOption
- **Field**: `repo: Option<RepoSelector>` (line 73)
- **Type**: Option<RepoSelector>

**Field name source:**

**Consumer sites:**

2. `/home/wink/data/prgs/rust/vc-x1/src/common.rs:736` (comment reference, not access)
3. Multiple instances in test files and other consumers using `params.repo` (which is the extracted params struct, not the leaf field itself)

---

#### LEAF 2: dry_run.rs — DEVIATES FROM CONVENTION

#### LEAF 4: account.rs — DEVIATES FROM CONVENTION

#### LEAF 5: config.rs — PARTIALLY DEVIATES (SPECIAL CASE)

### TASK 2: DOCUMENTATION — CONVENTION REFERENCES

**README.md lines 63–80** (Adding a new leaf section):
> "Single-field leaf — call the field `value` (it holds the parsed value-side of the option) and declare the flag explicitly with `#[arg(long = "<flag>", …)]`. The consumer then reads `args.<leaf>.value` rather than doubling the flag name (`args.squash.value`, not `args.squash.squash`). [...] The pre-existing single-field leaves (`scope`, `repo`, `dry_run`, `private`, `account`, `config`, `use_template`) predate this and still derive the flag from the field name; migrating them to `value` is tracked in [`../../TODO.md`](../../TODO.md)."

**README.md lines 145–149** (Consuming an OF section):
> "For **single-field leaves** (e.g. `SquashOption`, `DryRunFlag`, `PrivateFlag`), direct read at the consumer site (`args.squash.value`, or `args.provision.dry_run.dry_run` for the pre-`value` leaves) is fine — wrapping the inner value in a `&LeafType` parameter doesn't earn the indirection."

---

### SUMMARY TABLE

| Leaf | Struct | Field Name | Field Type | Flag Explicit? | Notes |
|------|--------|-----------|-----------|---|---|
| squash (exemplar) | SquashOption | `value` | Option<SquashSpec> | YES `long="squash"` | Follows convention |
| repo | RepoOption | `repo` | Option<RepoSelector> | NO (inferred) | Doubled: `.repo.repo` |
| dry_run | DryRunFlag | `dry_run` | bool | NO (inferred) | Doubled: `.dry_run.dry_run` |
| private | PrivateFlag | `private` | bool | NO (inferred) | Doubled: `.private.private` |
| account | AccountOption | `account` | Option<String> | NO (inferred) | Doubled: `.account.account` |
| config | ConfigOption | `raw` | Option<String> | YES `long="config"` | Mismatched: flag ≠ field |
| use_template | UseTemplateOption | `use_template` | Option<String> | NO (inferred) | Doubled: `.use_template.use_template` |
